summary: My mother's side of the family is from Japan, and she was actually born there. During World War II, she lived through the hardships in Japan, including food shortages that forced young people to gather apples from the mountains to survive. After the war, my father was stationed in Japan as part of the US occupation under General MacArthur, where he helped bridge cultures by translating and teaching American soldiers about Japanese customs.

My father's family immigrated to Hawaii in the early 1900s, where my grandfather worked as a tailor on Oahu. Most of my family, including my father and siblings, grew up in Honolulu during World War II. While many Japanese Americans on the mainland were interned, most in Hawaii were not, because the Japanese community was essential to the island's infrastructure. My father and his brother served in the military from Hawaii, showing their loyalty to the United States.

My family has a deep connection to the United States, especially my father, who was a second-generation Japanese American. Despite the challenges during World War II, including suspicion and discrimination, my father and uncle served proudly in the US military to prove their loyalty. We were always taught to be Americans first and to never embarrass our people. After the war, my father worked with the US occupation forces in Japan, helping with translation and cultural understanding.

World War II was a defining period for my family. The war separated us, with some siblings stuck in Japan and others in Hawaii. My father served in the Military Intelligence Service as a translator, while my uncle fought in the 100th Infantry Battalion. The war also brought hardship to my mother in Japan and led to tragic losses, like my uncle who died as part of the kamikaze unit in Okinawa. It was a time that shaped our values and sense of identity deeply.

My father was in Hawaii during the bombing of Pearl Harbor in 1941, already in his early twenties. That event was a turning point for our family and the Japanese American community, leading to increased suspicion and the need to prove our loyalty. It separated family members, as some were in Japan and others remained in Hawaii. The attack deeply influenced my father's decision to join the military and serve the United States.

My father served in the Military Intelligence Service during World War II, where he worked as a translator of Japanese messages for the American forces. Being bilingual, he played a crucial role in the Pacific Theater and later during the US occupation of Japan. His work helped facilitate communication and cultural understanding between the Japanese and American sides, which was vital during such a tense period.

My uncle, the younger brother of my father, was part of the 100th Infantry Battalion during World War II. This was a unit composed mainly of Japanese Americans who wanted to prove their loyalty to the United States. While my father served in Military Intelligence, my uncle fought bravely in combat. Their service was a source of pride for our family and the Japanese American community.

After World War II ended, my father was stationed in Japan as part of the US occupation under General Douglas MacArthur. During this time, he worked as a translator, helping to bridge the cultural gap between the American forces and the Japanese people. MacArthur's leadership was significant in shaping the post-war transition, and my father's role was important in facilitating smooth communication during this period.

An interesting connection in our family story involves a Japanese interpreter who helped my father locate his older sister in Japan after the war. This interpreter had been educated at New York University before returning to Japan during the war. It was a remarkable coincidence that someone with ties to NYU played a key role in reuniting family members separated by the conflict.

My father's oldest brother was drafted into the kamikaze unit during World War II. He was a college student at the time and was sent to fight in Okinawa. Tragically, he died there, and we never had the chance to meet him. His sacrifice is a painful part of our family history and reflects the harsh realities faced by many Japanese during the war.

Okinawa was where my uncle, who was part of the kamikaze unit, lost his life during World War II. It was a brutal battle and a tragic place for our family. We never got to meet him, but his death in Okinawa is a somber reminder of the war's devastating impact on Japanese families, including ours.
